summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the ecological footprint?

The ecological footprint measures how much biologically productive land and water is needed to produce the resources a person or population consumes and to absorb the waste they generate.

What is the tragedy of the commons?

The tragedy of the commons is an economic concept describing how shared resources are overexploited when each user acts in self-interest, ultimately degrading the resource for everyone.

What is an ecosystem service?

Ecosystem services are the benefits humans derive from natural ecosystems, including provisioning (food, water), regulating (climate, flood control), supporting (nutrient cycling), and cultural (recreation) services.

What is sustainable development?

Sustainable development integrates economic growth, environmental protection, and social equity so that present needs are met while preserving resources and opportunities for future generations.

What is a carbon footprint?

A carbon footprint quantifies the total greenhouse gas emissions caused directly and indirectly by an individual, organization, event, or product, usually expressed as tonnes of CO2 equivalent.

What is a circular economy?

A circular economy is an alternative to the linear take-make-dispose model, designed so that products, materials, and resources are maintained at their highest value for as long as possible.

What is greenwashing?

Greenwashing is a deceptive marketing practice where companies make unsubstantiated or misleading claims about their environmental practices to appeal to eco-conscious consumers without making real changes.

What is the precautionary principle?

The precautionary principle states that when an activity raises threats to the environment or human health, preventive action should be taken even if some cause-and-effect relationships are not fully established.

What is biocapacity?

Biocapacity measures the ability of ecosystems to produce useful biological materials and absorb waste, expressed in global hectares. When ecological footprint exceeds biocapacity, resources are being depleted.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: An example of a renewable resource is:

Solar energy is renewable because it is continuously replenished by the sun and will remain available on human time scales, unlike fossil fuels which take millions of years to form.

Example: The tragedy of the commons describes:

The tragedy of the commons, described by Garrett Hardin in 1968, occurs when individuals overexploit a shared resource for personal gain, leading to depletion that harms everyone.

Example: The United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) include how many goals?

The UN Sustainable Development Goals consist of 17 interconnected goals adopted in 2015 that address global challenges including poverty, inequality, climate change, and environmental degradation.

Example: What is the tragedy of the commons?

The tragedy of the commons is an economic concept describing how shared resources are overexploited when each user acts in self-interest, ultimately degrading the resource for everyone.

Example: A fishing community has relied on a local fishery for generations. In recent years, catches have declined dramatically because boats use larger nets and fish faster than populations can reproduce. This situation is a real-world example of:

This overfishing scenario exemplifies the tragedy of the commons: each fisher acts rationally to maximize their catch, but the collective result is depletion of the shared fish stock that everyone depends on.

Example: A smartphone manufacturer designs its new phone so that the battery, screen, and other parts can be individually replaced and eventually recycled into new phones. This business model is an example of:

Designing products for repairability and recyclability is a key circular economy strategy that extends product life, reduces waste, and keeps valuable materials circulating in the economy.
